sanskrit_devanagari:
देहिनोऽस्मिन्यथा देहे कौमारं यौवनं जरा ।
तथा देहान्तरप्राप्तिर्धीरस्तत्र न मुह्यति ॥ १३ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
dehino 'smin yathā dehe kaumāraṁ yauvanaṁ jarā |
tathā dehāntara-prāptir dhīras tatra na muhyati || 13 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.013 = MBh_06,024.013). GRETIL writes 'dehāntaraprāptir' as a compound; vedabase renders 'dehāntara-prāptiḥ' with hyphen; akṣara sequence identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: As the embodied soul continuously passes, in this body, from boyhood to youth to old age, the soul similarly passes into another body at death. A sober person is not bewildered by such a change.
Just as the embodied soul continuously passes from childhood to youth to old age, similarly, at the time of death, the soul passes into another body. The wise are not deluded by this.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Since every living entity is an individual soul, each is changing his body every moment, manifesting sometimes as a child, sometimes as a youth and sometimes as an old man. Yet the same spirit soul is there and does not undergo any change.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (per Advaita Ashrama edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: These three states are mutually distinct. On these, when the first state gets destroyed the Self does not get destroyed; when the second state comes into being It is not born... It is seen that the Self, which verily remains unchanged, acquires the second and third states.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as an analogical proof of Self-eternality: childhood, youth, old age are 'mutually distinct' states, each destroyed when the next arises, yet the Self that 'has' these states is not destroyed in any of the transitions. The same logic extends across body-death: the body is the next state; the Self is what holds across the transition. The dhīra (wise) is not bewildered because they have located the right level — the level at which the transitions occur, not the level that owns them.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: As the self is eternal, one does not grieve, thinking that the self is lost, when an embodied self living in a body gives up the state of childhood and attains youth and other states. Similarly, the wise men, knowing that the self is eternal, do not grieve, when the self attains a body different from the present body.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja takes the same analogical structure but adds: the eternal selves, due to beginningless karma, become embodied in bodies suited to their karma; the discipline of acting without attachment to fruits is the path to liberation from this cycle. The verse is read both as a Self-eternality proof (the wise are not bewildered by transitions because the substrate is real) and as the foundation for the karma-yoga teaching that follows.

positions.rendering: passes into another body at death
passes into another body at the time of death
acquisition of another body, a body different from the present one (no explicit 'at death' — the analogical structure is the focus)

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Modern translators add 'at death' to clarify the analogy for English readers. Shankara's rendering preserves the Sanskrit's restraint — the verse does not explicitly say 'at death', it says 'attainment of another body', and the analogical structure (childhood → youth → old age → next body) is the argument, not a doctrinal claim about the moment of transition. Translators differ in how aggressively they fill in the implied 'at death' from the surrounding context.

positions.rendering: an intelligent person — one who has located the right level of analysis
the steady, the firm, the wise

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Dhīra is technically narrower than 'wise' in general — it carries the connotation of steadiness, the operator who does not flinch. Prabhupada's 'sober person' picks up this steadiness; Mukundananda flattens to 'the wise'. The engineering analog should preserve the steadiness aspect: not 'one who knows', but 'one who is not destabilized by'.

literal_meaning: As, for the embodied one, in this body, [there are the states of] childhood, youth, and old age, so [there is] the attainment of another body. The steady-minded one is not bewildered regarding that.
traditional_meaning_consensus: The verse on state transitions. Krishna gives the analogical proof of Self-eternality: just as the embodied being passes through childhood, youth, and old age within one body without ceasing to be, so it passes to another body without ceasing to be. The dhīra — the steady-minded — is not bewildered by the transition, because they have grasped that what holds across the transitions is not the same level as what is being transformed. All major commentarial traditions agree on the analogical structure; differences are in emphasis (Shankara on Self-eternality and the unchanging substrate, Ramanuja on the eternal selves linked through karma), not on the verse's claim. The chapter's voice is calm here — Krishna has moved from rebuke (2.11) and ontological assertion (2.12) into the first analogical demonstration. The verse establishes the doctrine of state transitions: identity holds through state change.
